title: invAIriant Audit — overlaynet PR #351 (handshake + ordered apply)
scope: PR #351, which adds a session handshake with a fixed magic preamble to transport/handshake.go and changes transport/apply.go to apply incoming state updates in arrival order, re-applying on retransmit. Audited against docs/threat-model.md and docs/design/replication.md. Out of scope: the cryptographic key exchange (transport/noise.go), peer discovery, and the state CRDT internals.
participants: claude-agent (lens auditor: lamport, network-persistence, parnas, turing)

lens_scores.verdict: Recv applies updates in arrival order with no Seq check, and onTimeout re-applies on retransmit with no dedup, so out-of-order or duplicated delivery corrupts state (PT-001)
the handshake opens with a constant 6-byte magic preamble and fixed-size fixed-order fields, giving the transport a stable fingerprint a DPI classifier can match on the first packet (PT-002)
the transport/state boundary is clean; both defects live inside the transport module, not across its interfaces (obs O-1)
the resend path is bounded (single resend, no loop), so this is a correctness defect, not a liveness one (obs O-2)

findings.claim: The receive path assumes messages arrive in the order they were produced and that a message is delivered at most once: Recv applies updates in arrival order without a sequence check, and onTimeout re-applies the update on retransmit with no deduplication, so reordering or a retransmit-plus-original double-applies state.

evidence.description: Recv now calls s.state.Apply(msg) directly with an in-line comment 'assumes in-order arrival'; the prior ApplyOrdered(msg) that buffered and reordered by msg.Seq was removed
onTimeout calls s.state.Apply(msg) and then resend(msg); if the original also lands, Apply runs twice for one logical update — no processed-Seq guard

evidence.hunk:
-    // previously: buffered and reordered by msg.Seq before applying
-    return s.state.ApplyOrdered(msg)
+    s.state.Apply(msg)              // no Seq check; assumes in-order arrival
+    return nil

evidence.description: replication.md states 'state updates are applied in Seq order and are idempotent under retransmit'; the code applies in arrival order and re-applies on retransmit
transport/apply_test.go covers only in-order, single-delivery sequences; no test delivers messages out of Seq order or delivers the same Seq twice (retransmit + original)

findings.risk: Two peers are already a distributed system: any reordering by the network or a timeout-triggered retransmit that races its original diverges the two peers' state and cannot be reconciled from the message stream. Under real packet loss — the normal case for this overlay — the state silently corrupts.
findings.recommendation: Restore ordering and idempotency: buffer and apply by msg.Seq (reject or hold out-of-order arrivals), and guard Apply with a processed-Seq set so a retransmit-plus-original is a no-op on the second delivery. Update replication.md only if the guarantee changes. Add out-of-order and duplicate-delivery tests.

verification.method: read transport/apply.go:44-57; confirmed removal of ApplyOrdered and absence of any Seq/dedup guard; cross-read replication.md:37; grepped transport/apply_test.go for out-of-order/duplicate cases (none)
verification.notes: both the ordering assumption and the retransmit double-apply are present; one finding, two mechanisms

findings.claim: The new session handshake begins with a constant 6-byte magic preamble followed by fixed-size, fixed-order fields, giving the transport a stable per-session fingerprint a passive DPI classifier can match on the first packet — the transport is obfuscated-looking but not indistinguishable.

evidence.description: sessionMagic = []byte{0x4F,0x56,0x52,0x4C,0x59,0x01} ('OVRLY\x01') is written as the first bytes of every session, before the TLS-looking wrapper, then a fixed-size handshakeHeader{Ver, Peer} with static padding

threat-model.md claims 'session establishment is statistically indistinguishable from a standard TLS handshake and survives active probing'; a fixed magic constant in the clear is a trivially matchable signature
No fingerprint/indistinguishability test exists: nothing under transport/ compares the handshake byte distribution against a real TLS ClientHello or asserts the absence of a static preamble

findings.risk: A blocker running pattern matching blocks the entire overlay on the constant 'OVRLY\x01' preamble with one rule and near-zero false positives — reachability, the property the system exists for, collapses to a single string match. The threat-model's indistinguishability claim is false.
findings.recommendation: Remove the cleartext magic constant; derive session framing from the negotiated key so the first bytes are pseudorandom, and match legitimate TLS record framing rather than prefixing a marker. Add a fingerprint test comparing the handshake against a reference TLS ClientHello and asserting no static preamble; correct threat-model.md to the guarantee actually met.

verification.method: read transport/handshake.go:23-31; confirmed the constant preamble is written before any encryption; cross-read threat-model.md:28; searched transport/ for any statistical fingerprint test (none)
verification.notes: 'obfuscated' but not indistinguishable — a marker in the clear is a signature

observations.text: The transport/state boundary is clean: both defects live inside the transport module and are fixable without touching the state CRDT interface.
The resend path is bounded (a single resend, no retry loop), so PT-001 is a state-corruption defect rather than a liveness one; the turing lens finds no unbounded loop here.
observations.follow_up: Revisit if resend is ever changed to loop until acknowledged.

hypotheses.text: The removed ApplyOrdered means messages can now be lost entirely, not just reordered
hypotheses.rejected_reason: The transport still acks and the sender still retransmits on timeout (transport/apply.go:51-57 and transport/session.go:120-138), so loss is recovered; the defect is that recovery re-applies without a Seq guard (PT-001), not that messages vanish. Refuted by reading the resend path — captured inside PT-001, not as a separate loss finding.

hypotheses.text: The magic constant leaks the peer's identity to a passive observer
hypotheses.rejected_reason: sessionMagic is a fixed protocol marker identical for every peer (transport/handshake.go:23); it identifies the protocol, not the peer. The peer id is sent inside handshakeHeader after the marker but before encryption — a real concern, but a distinct one already implied by PT-002's 'first bytes should be pseudorandom' fix. Not filed as a separate unlinkability finding to avoid double-charging the same cleartext-handshake defect.

hypotheses.text: BigEndian binary.Write of the header is a portability bug across peer architectures
hypotheses.rejected_reason: binary.BigEndian is explicit and fixed on both write and read paths (transport/handshake.go:30 and transport/read.go:41), so byte order is consistent across architectures. No evidence of a mismatch; not a finding.

summary.executive_summary: PR #351 makes two changes that each defeat a core property of the overlay. First, transport/apply.go now applies state updates in arrival order and re-applies them on retransmit with no Seq check or dedup, so ordinary packet reordering or a timeout race corrupts distributed state that cannot be rebuilt from the stream (PT-001, S1 ORDERING_ASSUMPTION) — and replication.md explicitly promises Seq-ordered, idempotent apply. Second, the new handshake opens with a constant 'OVRLY\x01' magic preamble in the clear, a stable fingerprint a blocker blocks with one rule, while threat-model.md claims the handshake is indistinguishable from TLS (PT-002, S1 DISTINGUISHABLE_TRANSPORT). A generic review passes the happy path — messages arrive in order in the test, the handshake completes — and sees neither defect. The transport boundary is clean (parnas 7), but two S1s block the merge.
summary.weakest_reason: the receive path assumes a single well-ordered, at-most-once world (arrival-order apply, retransmit re-apply) with nothing enforcing it — the defining failure mode of this lens (PT-001)

required_actions.action: Apply state updates by msg.Seq (buffer/reject out-of-order) and guard Apply with a processed-Seq set so retransmit-plus-original is idempotent; add out-of-order and duplicate-delivery tests

Remove the cleartext magic preamble; derive pseudorandom session framing from the negotiated key and match TLS record framing; add a fingerprint test vs a reference ClientHello; correct threat-model.md
